title: Amate bark paper
materials: fig tree bark strips (Ficus species, stripped and soaked overnight) OR paper mulberry bark as a substitute
wood ash lye (water left after soaking wood ash, for cooking the bark) OR calcium hydroxide (lime) solution
flat smooth board as beating surface
sources: Amate papermaking tradition documented in Eric Wolf, Empire of the Aztec (general historical account). Pre-Columbian papermaking in Mesoamerica described in public-domain sources including US Bureau of American Ethnology reports (late 19th / early 20th century) — these federal publications are public domain.
notes: Amate is the pre-Columbian bark paper of Mexico, made from fig-tree or mulberry bark cooked in lime or ash-lye solution, then beaten flat. Still produced in Otomi communities in the state of Puebla, Mexico. Covers: the bark cook (ash-lye softens the bast fibres), rinsing thoroughly after cooking, the beating and overlapping: strips are laid in a grid pattern and beaten together on a smooth board (the fibres mat together), drying on the board in the sun or in a warm oven (low temperature). No mould, no water suspension: this is felting by beating, not paper-forming by sieving. Resulting sheet has a visible grid pattern from the bark strips.
